region_of_origin: India (North Indian / Mughlai tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori-marinated tiger prawns. FOODON_03302051 (shrimp food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for tandoori-marinated tiger prawns in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955003 (Prawn - food) is the closest defensible parent. No specific concept exists for tandoori preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A full serving of Tandoori Jheenga Jal Taran at Kinara Boat Quay consists of approximately 6–8 large tiger prawns (estimated 350 g total), marinated in spiced yoghurt and cooked in a clay oven, typically served with mint chutney and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Shrimp / Prawn, cooked, spiced (tandoori preparation); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central: Shrimp, cooked (FDC ID 175180) for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides the primary basis for cooked prawn / tandoori prawn macronutrient values. Tiger prawns are large penaeid shrimp; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC 175180) for micronutrient completeness. Tandoori marinade (yoghurt, spices, small amount of oil) contributes modest additional fat and sodium. Energy recalculated from macros using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Seafood and protein-dominant foods; analogous estimate from shrimp / prawn cooked category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori tiger prawns. Protein-dominant seafood dishes with minimal carbohydrate content are consistently classified as very low GI (GI ≤ 10) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. A GI of 5 is used as a defensible analogous estimate for cooked prawn / shrimp dishes, consistent with the GI of other high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood preparations. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (5 × 7.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.35, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade and spices
hypertension.guidance: Tandoori Jheenga contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from the spiced yoghurt marinade and added salt; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 5 and only 2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Tandoori Jheenga has a negligible glycemic impact and is a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided accompaniments such as naan or rice are accounted for separately.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol and sodium from prawn and marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tiger prawns are naturally high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 152 mg per 100 g) and the tandoori marinade adds sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume moderate portions and balance this dish with low-sodium, high-fibre accompaniments, noting that the low saturated fat content (0.9 g per 100 g) is a favourable attribute.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Jheenga Jal Taran (Full) is a North Indian clay-oven-grilled tiger prawn dish served at Kinara restaurant, Boat Quay, Singapore. Large tiger prawns (Penaeus monodon) are marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture containing red chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice, then cooked at high temperature in a traditional tandoor (clay oven), imparting a characteristic smoky char and vibrant red-orange colour. The texture is firm yet succulent, with a mildly spiced, tangy, and aromatic flavour profile. The full portion comprises approximately 6–8 large prawns (estimated 350 g). Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (approximately 20.5 g per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (approximately 2 g per 100 g), and moderate in fat (approximately 3.5 g per 100 g), making it a protein-dense, low-glycemic-index option. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to the marinade. The dish is naturally gluten-free and suitable for halal diets. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI and high protein content, though sodium intake should be monitored by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is a popular starter in North Indian fine-dining contexts in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is tandoori jheenga suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp, as no exact entry for tandoori tiger prawns exists in any approved primary source. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level match was found in an approved primary source.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported for cooked prawn preparations in either ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central sources. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in cooked prawn preparations per USDA FoodData Central — confirmed zero. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate for a full restaurant portion of 6–8 large tiger prawns; actual restaurant portion weight may vary. Sodium value of 420 mg per 100 g reflects the contribution of the spiced yoghurt marinade and added salt typical of tandoori preparations; the base prawn sodium is approximately 111 mg per 100 g per USDA data, with the remainder attributed to marinade. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tandoori tiger prawns was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 5 × 7.0 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 0.35, reported as 0.4. The dish name 'Jal Taran' is a Kinara restaurant signature descriptor meaning 'water ripple', referring to the presentation style. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and all micronutrients set to -1 where not reported in ICMR-NIN are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central FDC 175180 (shrimp, cooked); values marked -1 indicate not reported in the primary ICMR-NIN source and estimated from USDA where available.
